standard_description: Represent sample spaces for compound events using methods such as organized lists, tables and tree diagrams. For an event described in everyday language (e.g., “rolling double sixes”), identify the outcomes in the sample space which compose the event.
subject: Math

problems.text:
An experiment involves flipping a fair coin and rolling a fair six-sided die.
a.   List all possible outcomes of the experiment. Use an organized list, table, or tree diagram.
b.   What is the probability of getting a head and an even number?
c.   What is the probability of getting a tail and the number 4?
d.   What is the probability of getting a 5?

Task
Many games use dice which are six-sided and fair (meaning each face on the die is equally likely to land face up). Many games also use the sum of two dice rolled at the same time to determine movement of game pieces. However, not all dice are six-sided. Imagine a game in which two fair four-sided (tetrahedral) dice are rolled simultaneously. These dice are in the shape of a pyramid, and when a die is rolled, the outcome is determined by the side that lands face down. Suppose that for these two dice, the possible values (corresponding to the four sides of the die) that can be obtained from each die are as follows:
Die #1: 1, 2, 3, or 4
Die #2: 2, 4, 6, or 8
A certain game determines the movement of players' game pieces based on the SUM of the numbers on the face down sides when two dice are rolled. There are 10 distinct sum values that can occur, and some of those sums occur more often than others.
Using an organized list, table, tree diagram, or method of your choosing, develop a list of all 16 possible outcomes (for example, Die #1 = 1 and Die #2 = 2 for a sum of 3; Die #1 = 1 and Die #2 = 4 for a sum of 5; and so on).
From your work in part i, determine the 10 **distinct sum values** that are possible and calculate the probability of obtaining each sum value. Note: as mentioned above, some values will occur more frequently than others.
Using your work in part ii, answer the following questions:
What is the probability of obtaining a sum of 5?
What is the probability of obtaining a sum that is more than 5?
What is the probability of obtaining a sum that is at most 5?
What is the probability of obtaining a sum that is at least 5?
What is the probability of obtaining a sum that is no less than 5?
Now consider the case where the DIFFERENCE in the numbers on the face down sides when two dice are rolled is important to the game. Unless the two die values are the same (in which case the difference is 0), the difference for purposes of this game will always be computed as the larger number value rolled minus the smaller number value rolled. In this way, the difference value for any roll of the two dice will always be positive or 0.
Using an organized list, table, tree diagram, or method of your choosing, develop a list of all 16 possible outcomes (for example, Die #1 = 1 and Die #2 = 2 for a difference of 1; Die #1 = 1 and Die #2 = 4 for a difference of 3; and so on).
From your work in part i, determine the 8 distinct difference values that are possible and calculate the probability of obtaining each difference value. Note: as mentioned above, some values will occur more frequently than others.
Using your work in part e, answer the following questions:
What is the probability of obtaining a difference of 5?
What is the probability of obtaining a difference that is more than 5?
What is the probability of obtaining a difference that is less than or equal to 5?

Task
Angie, Bridget, Carlos, and Diego are seated at random around a square table, one person to a side. What is the theoretical probability that Angie and Carlos are seated opposite each other?

Problem 1
An experiment involves a spinner and a fair coin, shown below. The spinner will be spun one time, and the coin will be flipped one time.
###IMAGE0###
What are all of the possible outcomes for this experiment? Organize your information in a list, table, or tree diagram.
Problem 2
Imagine a game in which two fair four-sided dice are rolled at the same time. These dice are in the shape of a pyramid, and when a die is rolled, the outcome is determined by the side that lands face down. The possible values (corresponding to the four sides of the die) for each die are as follows:
Die #1:  1, 2, 3, or 4                           Die #2:   2, 4, 6, or 8
a.   A certain game determines the movement of players' game pieces based on the SUM of the numbers on the face down sides when two dice are rolled.
Using an organized list, table, or tree diagram, develop a list of all 16 possible outcomes.
Answer the following questions:
What is the probability of obtaining a sum of 5?
What is the probability of obtaining a sum that is more than 5?
What is the probability of obtaining a sum that is at most 5?
What is the probability of obtaining a sum that is at least 5?
b.   Another game determines movement of the game pieces based on the DIFFERENCE in the numbers on the face down sides. The difference for purposes of this game will always be computed as the larger number value rolled minus the smaller number value rolled. In this way, the difference will always be positive or 0.
Using an organized list, table, or tree diagram, develop a list of all 16 possible outcomes.
Answer the following questions:
What is the probability of obtaining a difference of 5?
What is the probability of obtaining a difference that is more than 5?
What is the probability of obtaining a difference that is less than or equal to 5?
Problem 3
At an ice cream store, a customer can make a sundae that includes ice cream, one candy topping, and one liquid topping. The options are listed below.
###TABLE0###
a.   How many different sundae combinations are possible, assuming both a candy and liquid topping are selected?
b.   Use a list, table, or tree diagram to show all possible outcomes.
c.   How many different sundaes include mint chip ice cream and hot fudge topping?
d.   Assuming each sundae combination is equally likely, what is the probability that the store’s next customer orders a sundae with vanilla ice cream and gummy bears?

khan_exercises.title: Sample spaces for compound events
khan_exercises.description: Practice checking if sample space diagrams match a compound event.
topic_path: Keeping track of all possible outcomes

items.question_content:
You're picking out water balloons. There are $3$ colors and $2$ sizes.   

**If you randomly pick the color and size, which of these tables lists all possible outcomes? (Each row represents one outcome.) 
**   

[[☃ radio 1]]


**Table A:**  

Color | Size
- | :-: | -
Green | Large
Green | Small
Orange | Large
Orange | Small
Yellow | Large
Yellow | Small

**Table B:**  

Color | Size
- | :-: | -
Green | Large
Orange | Large
Yellow | Large
Green | Small
Orange | Small
Yellow | Small
hints: We're choosing from $2$ sizes and $3$ colors, so there should be $2\times3=6$ total outcomes.
In a useful table, no two outcomes should be exactly the same.
These tables are organized differently, but they both list all $6$ possible outcomes.

items.question_content:
You're ordering a one-topping pizza. You can choose from $3$ different toppings and $2$ types of crust.

**If you randomly pick the topping and crust, which of these tables lists all possible outcomes? (Each row represents one outcome.) 
**  

[[☃ radio 1]]

**Table A:**  

Crust | Topping
- | :-: | -
Thin crust | Pepperoni
Thin crust | Cheese
Thin crust | Mushroom
Deep dish | Pepperoni
Deep dish | Cheese
Deep dish | Mushroom

**Table B:**  

Topping 1 | Topping 2
- | :-: | -
Pepperoni | Pepperoni
Pepperoni | Cheese
Pepperoni | Mushroom
Cheese | Cheese
Mushroom | Cheese
Mushroom | Mushroom
hints: We're choosing from $2$ crusts and $3$ toppings, so there should be $2\times3=6$ total outcomes.

Only one of the tables actually shows different crust possibilities. The other table shows some possibilities for the toppings on a two-topping pizza.

**Table A is the correct table.**

items.question_content:
You've decided to take $3$ steps and randomly choose left or right as the direction each time.

**Which of these tables lists all possible outcomes of your random walk? (Each row represents one outcome.) 
**  

[[☃ radio 1]]

**Table A:**  

First | Second | Third
- | :-: | :-: | -
Left | Left | Left
Left | Left | Right
Left | Right | Left
Left | Right | Right
Right | Left | Left
Right | Left | Right
Right | Right | Left
Right | Right | Right

**Table B:**

First | Second | Third
- | :-: | :-: | -
Right | Right | Right
Left | Right | Right
Right | Left | Right
Left | Left | Right
Right | Right | Left
Left | Right | Left
Right | Left | Left
Left | Left | Left
hints: There are $2$ possible outcomes for each step, so there should be $2\times2\times2=8$ outcomes in a useful table.
In a useful table, no two outcomes should be exactly the same.
These tables are organized differently, but they both list all $8$ possible outcomes.

items.question_content:
**Which of these tables lists all the possible outcomes of flipping $3$ coins? (Each row represents one outcome.) 
**   

[[☃ radio 1]]


**Table A:**  

First | Second | Third
- | :-: | :-: | -
H | T | T
H | T | T
H | H | T
H | H | T
T | H | H
T | H | H
T | T | H
T | T | H


**Table B:**  

First | Second | Third
- | :-: | :-: | -
H | H | H
H | H | T
H | T | H
H | T | T
T | H | H
T | H | T
T | T | H
T | T | T
hints: There are $2$ possible outcomes for each step, so there should be $2\times2\times2=8$ outcomes in a useful table.
In a useful table, no two outcomes should be exactly the same.
Table A has $8$ rows, but it has some repeated outcomes, so it doesn't actually show all of the possible outcomes.
Only Table B lists all $8$ possible outcomes.

items.question_content:
Desmond lives in Melbourne and is taking a trip to Sydney. He can travel to Sydney and back in $3$ different ways: by bus, cab, or train. Desmond chooses how to get to Sydney and back at random.   

**Which of these tree diagrams can be used to find all of the different ways that Desmond can get to Sydney and back?**  

[[☃ radio 1]]

$\text{B}= \text{Bus} \\ 
\text{C}= \text{Cab} \\ 
\text{T}= \text{Train} $ 

Diagram A:  
[[☃ image 1]]

Diagram B:  
[[☃ image 2]]
hints:
There are $3$ possible outcomes when Desmond randomly chooses how he'll get to Sydney. Here's what a tree diagram would look like for this first choice:

[[☃ image 1]]

There are also $3$ possible outcomes when Desmond chooses how to get back to Melbourne. Each branch splits into $3$ more branches, so there are $3\times 3=9$ possibilities. 

**Diagram B is the useful diagram for this situation.**

[[☃ image 1]]

options.alt: A tree diagram. The starting point branches to nodes B and B. Nodes B and B each branch to a pair of C and C nodes. The four C and C nodes each branch into T and T end nodes.
A tree diagram. The starting point branches to nodes B, C, and T. Nodes B, C, and T each branch to a B, C, and T ending nodes.

items.question_content:
You've decided to take $3$ steps and randomly choose left or right as the direction each time.

**Which of these tree diagrams can be used to find the probability of taking any possible combination of $2$ lefts and $1$ right?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

Diagram A:  
[[☃ image 1]]

  

Diagram B:  
[[☃ image 2]]
hints:
There are $2$ possible outcomes for the first step. Here's what a tree diagram would look like for this first choice:

[[☃ image 1]]

There are also $2$ possible outcomes for the second step. Each branch splits into $2$ more branches, so for two random steps, there are $2\times 2 = 4$ possibilities. 

[[☃ image 1]]

On the third step, the tree splits again, so there are $2\times 2 \times 2 = 8$ total possibilities.

[[☃ image 1]]

Diagram B can be used to find the probability of taking $2$ lefts and $1$ right because the diagram shows all of the different ways that the event can occur. Diagram A doesn't allow us to find the probability because it shows only events, not all of the possible ways that a particular event can occur.

options.alt: A tree diagram. The starting point branches to end nodes 3 Lefts, 2 Lefts 1 Right, 1 Left 2 Rights, and 3 Rights.
A tree diagram. The starting point branches to nodes L and R. Nodes L and R each branch to another pair of L and R nodes. Those four nodes each branch into their own pair of ending nodes of L and R.

items.question_content:
You've decided to flip $3$ coins.

**Which of these tree diagrams can be used to find the probability of getting any possible combination of $2$ heads and $1$ tail?** 

[[☃ radio 1]]

Diagram A:  
[[☃ image 2]]

Diagram B:  
[[☃ image 1]]
hints:
There are two possible outcomes for each individual coin flip. Here's what a tree diagram would look like for one flip:

[[☃ image 1]]

If we flip two coins, there are two possibilities for the first coin flip and two for the second flip. The tree splits at each flip, so there are a total of $2\times2=4$ possibilities. 

[[☃ image 1]]

If we flip three coins, the tree splits again, so there are $2\times2\times2=8$ possible outcomes.

[[☃ image 1]]

Diagram A can be used to find the probability of getting $2$ heads and $1$ tail because the diagram shows all of the different ways that the event can occur. Diagram B doesn't allow us to find the probability because it shows only events, not all of the possible ways that a particular event can occur.

options.alt: A tree diagram. The starting point branches to 4 nodes, which read 3 heads; 2 heads and 1 tail; 1 head and 2 tails; and 3 tails.

A tree diagram. The starting point branches to nodes H and T. Nodes H and T each branch to another pair of H and T nodes. Those four nodes each branch into their own pair of endpoints H and T with 8 nodes.

items.question_content:
You're ordering a one-topping pizza. You can choose from $3$ different toppings and $2$ types of crust.

**If you randomly pick the topping and crust, which of these diagrams can be used to find all of the different kinds of pizza you can order?**  

[[☃ radio 1]]

$\text{P}= \text{Pepperoni} \\ 
\text{C}= \text{Cheese} \\ 
\text{M}= \text{Mushroom} $  

$\text{T}= \text{Thin crust} \\ 
\text{D}= \text{Deep dish crust} $

Diagram A:  
[[☃ image 1]]

Diagram B:  
[[☃ image 2]]
hints:
If we choose the topping first, the top layer of the diagram will look like this:

[[☃ image 1]]

If we then choose the crust, the full diagram will look like this:

[[☃ image 1]]

We could also choose the crust first. Then the top layer of the diagram will look like this:

[[☃ image 1]]

Choosing the topping second, the full diagram will look like this:

[[☃ image 1]]

These diagrams are organized differently, but they show the same $6$ possible outcomes. Both diagrams are valid choices.

options.alt: A tree diagram. The starting point branches to nodes T and D. Nodes T and D each branch to P, C, and M end nodes.
A tree diagram. The starting point branches to nodes P, M, and C. Nodes P, M, and C each branch to T and D end nodes.

items.question_content:
You've decided to buy a potted plant to brighten up your room. The gardening store near you offers $4$ different plants that can live inside and $4$ different types of pots.

|| **Diagram A** ||
| Clay pot |  Plastic pot |  Metal pot |  Wood pot
	-|-|-|-
**Tulip** | 	Clay pot, Tulip | Plastic pot, Tulip | Metal pot, Tulip | Wood pot, Tulip
**Fern** | 	Clay pot, Fern | Plastic pot, Fern | Metal pot, Fern | Wood pot, Fern
**Cactus** | 	Clay pot, Cactus | Plastic pot, Cactus | Metal pot, Cactus | Wood pot, Cactus
**Ficus** | 	Clay pot, Ficus | Plastic pot, Ficus | Metal pot, Ficus | Wood pot, Ficus

|| **Diagram B** ||
| Tulip |  Fern |  Cactus |  Ficus
	-|-|-|-
**Clay pot** | 	Clay pot, Tulip | Clay pot, Fern | Clay pot, Cactus | Clay pot, Ficus
**Plastic pot** | 	Plastic pot, Tulip | Plastic pot, Fern | Plastic pot, Cactus | Plastic pot, Ficus
**Metal pot** | 	Metal pot, Tulip | Metal pot, Fern | Metal pot, Cactus | Metal pot, Ficus
**Wood pot** | 	Wood pot, Tulip | Wood pot, Fern | Wood pot, Cactus | Wood pot, Ficus

**If you randomly choose the plant and the pot, which of these diagrams can be used to find all of the possible outcomes?**  

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: There are $4$ different plants and $4$ different pots, so there should be $4\times4=16$ possible outcomes.
In a useful diagram, no two outcomes should be exactly the same.
The rows and columns have traded places in these two diagrams, but they both show all $16$ possible outcomes.

options.alt: A table with four columns and four rows. The columns are labeled Clay pot, Plastic pot, Metal pot, and Wood pot. The rows are labeled Tulip, Fern, Cactus, and Ficus. There are 16 different outcomes.
A table with four columns and four rows. The columns are labeled Tulip, Fern, Cactus, and Ficus. The rows are labeled Clay pot, Plastic pot, Metal pot, and Wood pot. There are 16 different outcomes.

items.question_content:
You're at a clothing store that dyes your clothes while you wait. The store offers $4$ different articles of clothing and $3$ colors.

|| **Diagram A** ||
|Blue |  Orange |  Purple
-|-|-|-
**Socks** | 	Blue Socks | Orange Socks | Purple Socks
**Hat** | 	Blue Hat | Orange Hat | Purple Hat
**Pants** | 	Blue Pants | Orange Pants | Purple Pants
**Shirt** | 	Blue Shirt | Orange Shirt | Purple Shirt

|| **Diagram B** ||
| Purple |  Blue |  Orange
	-|-|-|-
**Shirt** | 	Purple Shirt | Blue Shirt | Orange Shirt
**Pants** | 	Purple Pants | Blue Pants | Orange Pants
**Socks** | 	Purple Socks | Blue Socks | Orange Socks
**Hat** | 	Purple Hat | Blue Hat | Orange Hat

**If you randomly choose the article of clothing and the color, which of these diagrams can be used to find all of the possible outcomes?**  

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: There are $4$ different articles of clothing and $3$ different colors, so there should be $4\times3=12$ possible outcomes.
In a useful diagram, no two outcomes should be exactly the same.
The rows and columns are organized differently in these two diagrams, but they both show all $12$ possible outcomes.

options.alt: A table with 3 columns and 4 rows. The columns are labeled Blue, Orange, and Purple. The rows are labeled Sock, Hat, Pant, and Shirt. There are 12 different outcomes.
A table with 3 columns and 4 rows. The columns are labeled Purple, Blue, and Orange. The rows are labeled Shirt, Pant, Sock, and Hat. There are 12 different outcomes.

items.question_content:
You're picking out water balloons. There are $3$ colors and $2$ sizes.

**If you randomly pick the color and size, which of these diagrams can be used to find all of the possible outcomes?**  

[[☃ radio 1]]

$\text{G}= \text{Gold} \\ 
\text{O}= \text{Orange} \\ 
\text{Y}= \text{Yellow} $

$\text{L}= \text{Large} \\ 
\text{S}= \text{Small} $ 

Diagram A:  
[[☃ image 1]]

Diagram B:  
[[☃ image 2]]
hints:
If we choose the color first, the top layer of the diagram will look like this:

[[☃ image 1]]

If we then choose the size, the full diagram will look like this:

[[☃ image 1]]

We could also choose the size first. Then the top layer of the diagram will look like this:

[[☃ image 1]]

Choosing the color second, the full diagram will look like this:

[[☃ image 1]]

These diagrams are organized differently, but they show the same $6$ possible outcomes. Both diagrams are valid choices.

options.alt: A tree diagram. The starting point branches to nodes G, O, and Y. Nodes G, O, and Y each branch to L and S end nodes.
A tree diagram. The starting point branches to nodes L and S. Nodes L and S each branch to G, O, and Y end nodes.

items.question_content:
Lucy and Katy are registering for a sports league for next year.  There are $2$ sports (volleyball and basketball) and $3$ seasons (fall, winter, and spring) to choose from.  They each created a display to represent the sample space of randomly picking a sport and a season.

|| **Lucy's display**  ||
Sport | Season 
- | - | -
Volleyball | Fall
Volleyball | Winter
Volleyball | Spring 
Basketball | Fall
Basketball | Winter
Basketball | Spring 

|| **Katy's display**  ||
| Volleyball |  Basketball
	-|-|-|-
Fall | 	Volleyball, Fall | Basketball, Fall 
Winter | 	Volleyball, Winter | Basketball, Winter
Spring | 	Volleyball, Spring | Basketball, Spring
Summer | 	Volleyball, Summer | Basketball, Summer

**Whose display correctly represents the sample space?**
[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: There are $\blue2$ different sports and $\pink3$ different seasons, so there are $\blue2\times\pink3=6$ possible outcomes.
Lucy's display is correct because there are $6$ possible outcomes and no two outcomes are exactly the same.
Katy's display is *not* correct because it has more than $6$ possible outcomes.
Lucy's display is correct.

items.question_content:
Ed and Sally are taking a tour of Paris.  There are $3$ different types of tours (walking tour, bike tour, and trolley tour) and $2$ different times each tour is offered (morning and evening).  They each created a display to represent the sample space of randomly picking a type of tour and a time.

|| **Ed's display**  ||
| Walking tour |  Bike tour  | Trolley tour
	-|-|-|-
**Morning** | 	Walking tour, Morning | Bike tour, Morning | Trolley tour, Morning
**Afternoon** | 	Walking tour, Afternoon | Bike tour, Afternoon | Trolley tour, Afternoon
**Evening** | 	Walking Tour, Evening | Bike tour, Evening | Trolley tour, Evening

**Sally's display:**  

$\text{W}= \text{Walking tour} \\
\text{B}= \text{Bike tour} \\ 
\text{T}= \text{Trolley tour} $ 

$\text{M}= \text{Morning} \\ 
\text{E}= \text{Evening} $


$\quad$[[☃ image 3]]

**Whose display correctly represents the sample space?**
[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: There are $\blue3$ different types of tours offered and $\pink2$ different times, so there are $\blue3\times\pink2=6$ possible outcomes.
Ed's display is *not* correct because it has more than $6$ possible outcomes.
Sally's display is correct because there are $6$ possible outcomes and no two outcomes are exactly the same.
Sally's display is correct.

options.alt: A tree diagram. The starting point branches to nodes W, B, and T. Nodes W, B, and T each branch to a pair of M and E nodes.

items.question_content:
Elroy and Rose are at Schematic to buy a school planner.  There are $3$ colors (purple, red, and blue) and $2$ formats (lined and unlined) offered. They each created a display to represent the sample space of randomly picking a color and a format.


**Whose display correctly represents the sample space?**
[[☃ radio 1]]


**Elroy's display:**  

$\text{P}= \text{Purple} \\
\text{R}= \text{Red} \\ 
\text{B}= \text{Blue} $ 

$\text{L}= \text{Lined} \\ 
\text{U}= \text{Unlined} $

[[☃ image 1]]

**Rose's display:**  


Color | Format 
- | - | -
Purple | Lined
Purple | Unlined
Red | Lined
Red | Unlined
Blue | Lined
Blue | Unlined
hints: There are $\blue3$ different colors and $\pink2$ different formats, so there are $\blue3\times\pink2=6$ possible outcomes.
Elroy's display is correct because there are $6$ possible outcomes and no two outcomes are exactly the same.
Rose's display is also correct because there are $6$ possible outcomes and no two outcomes are exactly the same.
Both displays are correct.

options.alt: A tree diagram. The starting point branches to nodes P, R, and B. Nodes P, R, and B each branch to L and U end nodes. There are 6 end nodes.

items.question_content:
Chase and Emily are buying stools for their patio. They are deciding between $3$ heights (table height, bar height, and XL height) and $3$ colors (brown, white, and black). They each created a display to represent the sample space of randomly picking a height and a color.

|| **Chase's display** ||
Height | Color 
-| -| 
Table Height | Brown
Table Height | Brown
Table Height | White
Bar Height | White
Bar Height | Black
Bar Height | Black
XL Height | White
XL Height | Black
XL Height | Black

|| **Emily's display** ||
| Table height |  Brown 
-|-|-|-
**Bar height** | 	Table height, Bar height | Brown, Bar height
**XL height** | 	Table height, XL height | Brown, XL height
**White** | 	Table height, White | Brown, White
**Black** | 	Table height, Black | Brown, Black
**Whose display correctly represents the sample space?**
[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: In a correct diagram, no two outcomes are exactly the same.
There are $\blue3$ different heights and $\pink3$ different colors, so there are $\blue3\times\pink3=9$ possible outcomes.
Chase's display is *not* correct because it has at least two outcomes that are exactly the same.
Emily's display is *not* correct because it has fewer than $9$ possible outcomes.
Neither display is correct.

options.alt: A table with two columns and four rows. The first column is labeled Table Height. The second column is labeled Brown. The first row is labeled Bar Height. The second row is labeled X L Height. The third row is labeled White. The fourth row is labeled Black. Inside the table the first cell is labeled Table Height, Bar Height. The second cell, Brown, Bar Height. The second row first cell, Table Height, X L Height. The second cell, Brown, X L Height. The third row first cell, Table Height, White. The second cell, Brown, White. The fourth row first cell, Table Height, Black. The second cell Brown, Black.

items.question_content:
Sonya and Jade are traveling from Barcelona to Madrid. They are deciding between $3$ different types of transportation (bus, train, and plane) and $2$ different times (morning and evening). They each created a display to represent the sample space of randomly picking a type of transportation and a time.


**Whose display correctly represents the sample space?**
[[☃ radio 1]]


**Sonya's display:**  

| Morning |  Evening
	-|-|-|-
**Bus** | Morning, Bus | Evening, Bus 
**Train** | Morning, Train | Evening, Train
**Plane** | Morning, Plane | Evening, Plane



**Jade's display:**  

$\text{B}= \text{Bus} \\
\text{T}= \text{Train} \\ 
\text{P}= \text{Plane} $ 

$\text{M}= \text{Morning} \\ 
\text{E}= \text{Evening} $

[[☃ image 2]]
hints: In a correct diagram, no two outcomes are exactly the same.
There are $\blue3$ different types of transportation and $\pink2$ different times, so there are $\blue3\times\pink2=6$ possible outcomes.
Sonya's display is correct because there are $6$ possible outcomes and no two outcomes are exactly the same.
Jade's display is *not* correct because it has more than $6$ possible outcomes.
Sonya's display is correct.

options.alt: A table with 2 columns and 3 rows. The columns are labeled Morning and Evening. The rows are labeled Bus, Train, and Plane. There are 6 different outcomes.
A tree diagram. The starting point branches to nodes B, T, and P. Nodes B, T, and P each branch to B, T, and P end nodes. There are 9 end nodes.

items.question_content:
Nour and Rana are shopping for a Christmas tree. They are deciding between $2$ different types of trees (real and fake) and $4$ colors for the ornaments (white, silver, gold, and purple). They each created a display to represent the sample space of randomly picking a type of tree and a color for the ornaments.


**Whose display correctly represents the sample space?**
[[☃ radio 1]]


**Nour's display:**  

$\text{R}= \text{Real} \\ 
\text{F}= \text{Fake} $

$\text{W}= \text{White} \\
\text{S}= \text{Silver} \\ 
\text{G}= \text{Gold} \\
\text{P}= \text{Purple} $

$\quad$[[☃ image 1]]

**Rana's display:**  

Tree type | Ornament color 
- | - | -
Real | White
Real | Silver
Real | Gold 
Real | Purple
Fake | White
Fake | Silver
Fake | Gold 
Fake | Purple
hints: There are $\blue2$ different types of trees and $\pink4$ different ornament colors, so there are $\blue2\times\pink4=8$ possible outcomes.
Nour's display is correct because there are $8$ possible outcomes and no two outcomes are exactly the same.
Rana's display is also correct because there are $8$ possible outcomes and no two outcomes are exactly the same.
Both displays are correct.

options.alt: A tree diagram. The starting point branches to nodes R and F. Nodes R and F each branch to W, S, G, and P end nodes.

items.question_content:
You're playing a fantasy game that allows you to create your own character. There are $5$ options for the race of the character, and there are $3$ options for the character's main tool.

|| **Diagram A** ||
| Magic |  Sword |  Slingshot
-|-|-|-
**Elf** | 	Magic, Elf | Sword, Elf | Slingshot, Elf
**Hobbit** | 	Magic, Hobbit | Sword, Hobbit | Slingshot, Hobbit
**Human** | 	Magic, Human | Sword, Human | Slingshot, Human
**Orc** | 	Magic, Orc | Sword, Orc | Slingshot, Orc
**Troll** | 	Magic, Troll | Sword, Troll | Slingshot, Troll

|| **Diagram B** ||
| Elf |  Hobbit |  Human
-|-|-|-
**Magic** | 	Magic, Elf | Magic, Hobbit | Magic, Human
**Sword** | 	Sword, Elf | Sword, Hobbit | Sword, Human
**Shield** | 	Shield, Elf | Shield, Hobbit | Shield, Human
**Slingshot** | 	Slingshot, Elf | Slingshot, Hobbit | Slingshot, Human
**Umbrella** | 	Umbrella, Elf | Umbrella, Hobbit | Umbrella, Human

**If you randomly choose the race and the tool, which of these diagrams can be used to find all of the possible outcomes?**  

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: There are $3$ different tools and $5$ different races, so there should be $3\times5=15$ possible outcomes.
Both diagrams have the correct number of unique outcomes, but they are different in another key way.
Diagram B has $3$ columns for races and $5$ rows for tools. A useful diagram needs $5$ races and $3$ tools.
Diagram A has all the needed features.

options.alt: A table with 3 columns and 5 rows. The columns are labeled Magic, Sword, and Slingshot. The rows are labeled Elf, Hobbit, Human, Orc, and Troll.
A table with 3 columns and 5 rows. The columns are labeled Elf, Hobbit, and Human. The rows are labeled Magic, Sword, Shield, Slingshot, and Umbrella.

items.question_content:
Harry  goes to Hogwarts School of Witchcraft and Wizardry. He can travel to school and back in $3$ different ways: by the Hogwarts Express, a flying car, or the Knight Bus. He's decided to choose his methods of transportation to and from Hogwarts at random this year.

**Which of these tables lists all the different ways Harry can get to Hogwarts and back? (Each row represents one outcome.) 
**  

[[☃ radio 1]]


**Table A:**  

To Hogwarts | From Hogwarts
- | :-: | -
Knight Bus | Knight Bus
Knight Bus | Flying Car
Knight Bus | Hogwarts Express
Flying Car | Knight Bus
Flying Car | Flying Car
Flying Car | Hogwarts Express
Hogwarts Express | Knight Bus
Hogwarts Express | Flying Car
Hogwarts Express | Hogwarts Express

**Table B:**

To Hogwarts | From Hogwarts
- | :-: | -
Knight Bus | Hogwarts Express
Flying Car | Flying Car
Hogwarts Express | Knight Bus
Knight Bus | Knight Bus
Flying Car | Hogwarts Express
Hogwarts Express | Flying Car
Knight Bus | Flying Car
Flying Car | Knight Bus
Hogwarts Express | Hogwarts Express
hints: There are $3$ possible outcomes for each trip, so there should be $3\times3=9$ outcomes in a useful table.
In a useful table, no two outcomes should be exactly the same.
These tables are organized differently, but they both list all $9$ possible outcomes.

items.question_content:
It's make-your-own-dish night at your favorite pasta restaurant. The restaurant offers $5$ types of pasta and $4$ different sauces. You get to choose one of each.

|| **Diagram A** ||
|Tomato sauce |  Cheese sauce |  Fettuccine |  Olive Oil
-|-|-|-
**Spaghetti** | 	Tomato, Spaghetti | Cheese, Spaghetti | Fettuccine, Spaghetti | Olive Oil, Spaghetti
**Bow ties** | 	Tomato, Bow ties | Cheese, Bow ties | Fettuccine, Bow ties | Olive Oil, Bow ties
**Meat sauce** | 	Tomato, Meat | Cheese, Meat | Meat, Fettuccine | Olive Oil, Meat
**Ravioli** | 	Tomato, Ravioli | Cheese, Ravioli | Fettuccine, Ravioli | Olive Oil, Ravioli
**Macaroni** | 	Tomato, Macaroni | Cheese, Macaroni | Fettuccine, Macaroni | Olive Oil, Macaroni

|| **Diagram B** ||
| Tomato sauce |  Cheese sauce |  Meat sauce |  Olive oil
-|-|-|-
**Spaghetti** | 	Tomato, Spaghetti | Cheese, Spaghetti | Meat, Spaghetti | Olive oil, Spaghetti
**Bow ties** | 	Tomato, Bow ties | Cheese, Bow ties | Meat, Bow ties | Olive oil, Bow ties
**Fettuccine** | 	Tomato, Fettuccine | Cheese, Fettuccine | Meat, Fettuccine | Olive oil, Fettuccine
**Ravioli** | 	Tomato, Ravioli | Cheese, Ravioli | Meat, Ravioli | Olive oil, Ravioli
**Macaroni** | 	Tomato, Macaroni | Cheese, Macaroni | Meat, Macaroni | Olive oil, Macaroni

**If you randomly choose the pasta and the sauce, which of these diagrams can be used to find all of the possible outcomes?**  

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: There are $4$ different sauces and $5$ different types of pasta, so there should be $4\times5=20$ possible outcomes.
Both diagrams have the correct number of unique outcomes, but they are different in another key way.

In diagram A, most of the rows correspond to types of pasta, and most of the columns correspond to types of sauces. 

"Meat sauce" and "Fettuccine" are in the wrong places, so some of the outcomes in diagram A have two sauces or two pastas mixed together. That's against the rules!
Diagram B shows all $20$ possible combinations of one pasta type and one sauce.
